answer:
**Widsith**, the wandering scop (poet) in the Old English poem of the same name (from *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14), catalogues an extensive array of lords, tribes, and peoples he has known or served across the world.[^1] A representative section is his list of rulers who governed various nations:

> Many men I have heard of who ruled over nations! [...] Of these, Hwala was for a time the best, and Alexander the most powerful of all mankind [...]. Attila ruled the Huns, Eormanric the Goths, Becca the Banings, the Burgundians Gifica. Caesar ruled the Greeks and Celic the Finns, [...] Fin Folcwalding the Frisian kin.[^2]

He follows with a list of his own travels: "I was with the Huns and with the Hreth-Goths, with the Swedes and with the Geats [...]. With the Saxons I was and the Sycgs [...]. With the Israelites I was and with the Assyrians, with the Hebrews and with the Indians and with the Egyptians."[^3]

This cataloguing establishes Widsith's authority and vast experience as a far-traveled performer, justifying his receipt of treasures and his right to praise generous lords. It underscores the scop's role in preserving heroic memory and fame: poets roam "through many lands," speak "their need" and "thanks," and ensure that "he who wins praise has under the heavens high and lasting renown."[^4]

**Iqbal's "Tarana-e-Hindi"** (rendered here as *Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet* in *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 26) catalogues the storied geography, history, and spiritual landmarks of Hindustan to evoke patriotic unity.[^5] The entire short poem is a refrain-like list across four stanzas; a representative opening:

> Where Chishti first Truth's holy call conveyed,  
> And Nanak's song of Oneness was displayed,  
> The land the Tatars for their homeland made,  
> Which drew Hijaz's sons from desert shade,  
> This is my homeland, this land of mine.[^6]

Subsequent stanzas invoke Greek wisdom, Persian stars, Turkish diamonds, Moses, Noah, and Sinai, all claimed as part of "this land of mine."[^7]

For the speaker (Iqbal voicing a collective "we" for Indian children), cataloguing fuses diverse Islamic, Hindu, biblical, and global heritages into a singular, exalted homeland. It instills pride, belonging, and paradise-like sanctity, transforming geography into an emotional and spiritual possession: living there is "a paradise of pure delight."

The corpus lacks *Tarana-e-Milli* (ch. 23 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*) as a geography-of-Hindostan match; that poem catalogues a pan-Islamic realm "from Chin to Arab lands."[^8]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 14 (/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/), [p-2f6bd3](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-2f6bd3).
[^2]: Ibid., [p-545201](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-545201).
[^3]: Ibid., [p-8276c0](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-8276c0).
[^4]: Ibid., [p-6bde05](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/14-widsith-matholade-wordhord-onleac/translation/#p-6bde05).
[^5]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 26 (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/).
[^6]: Ibid., [p-da6786](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/#p-da6786).
[^7]: Ibid. (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/#p-da6786).
[^8]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 23 (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/23-tarana-e-milli/translation/), [p-8a4681](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/23-tarana-e-milli/translation/#p-8a4681).
